preFix.title: Managing outdoor pigs in winter mud
preFix.subtitle: Bedding, drainage, and rotational grazing to keep pigs healthy when the paddock turns to slurry.
preFix.excerpt: Outdoor pigs on a small holding without hard-standing or adequate drainage will destroy a paddock in a wet autumn and be knee-deep in mud by November. Managing winter conditions well is the difference between a flock with good feet, dry bedding, and a paddock that recovers in spring, and one that degrades into an unmanageable slurry pit.
preFix.sourceNotes: AHDB Pork, Outdoor Pig Production winter management guidance. DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Pigs, 2003. SAC Consulting Scotland, Outdoor Pig Keeping in Wet Conditions.

content.text: Two weaners on half an acre of light, well-drained soil in a dry autumn will graze and root without destroying the land. The same two pigs on heavy clay after a wet October will have broken through the turf and begun working the topsoil within days. Winter mud management is mostly about accepting the reality early and acting before the paddock is completely degraded.
If you have more than one paddock, designate a
for winter use from October. Move the pigs onto it before the wet season starts. The sacrifice paddock will be heavily
by spring but the other paddocks will be protected and able to recover. After slaughter in autumn, rest the sacrifice paddock and reseed in April.

The pig arc must be kept dry inside regardless of outside conditions. In wet weather, add fresh straw daily rather than every few days; wet bedding drops in temperature quickly and chills pigs overnight. Position the arc on the highest, driest point of the paddock with the entrance facing away from the prevailing wind and rain. If the arc is sited on wet ground, place it on a pallets base or raise it on timber runners to prevent the floor sweating and rotting.

Feed should be given from a solid trough or feeder on firm ground or a concrete pad if available. Scattered grain in deep mud is wasted and contributes to the development of a muddy, diseased feed area. A simple concrete block or two timber pallets laid flat under the trough keeps the feeding area firmer than the surrounding ground and makes cleaning easier.

Deep mud softens the pig's hooves and can cause overgrowth and lameness if the pigs are unable to move on harder surfaces. Check all four hooves weekly in winter. Overgrown claws can be trimmed with hoof shears; a pig that is consistently favouring a leg needs a vet assessment for foot rot or joint infection.

initialFindings.message: TECHNIQUE tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
